nativeRange.habitat: Shallow reef lagoons and rubble zones with moderate flow and nutrient-rich conditions; commonly found alongside Xenia and Clavularia species

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via symbiotic zooxanthellae. Supplemental target feeding of fine zooplankton or phytoplankton is generally considered optional and may accelerate growth, but most specimens appear to thrive on photosynthesis alone under adequate reef lighting.

goodWith: other peaceful corals with adequate physical separation
avoidWith: slow-growing LPS corals in direct contact
any coral colony within encrusting reach without physical barrier

summary: Briareum violaceum (Green Star Polyp) is a fast-spreading encrusting soft coral with vivid green polyps atop a purple-grey mat. Widely considered beginner-friendly; tolerates variable water chemistry but tends to aggressively overgrow neighbors.
careNotes:
Taxonomic note: Briareum violaceum (Quoy & Gaimard, 1833) is the currently accepted name for what the aquarium trade long called Pachyclavularia violacea. The World Register of Marine Species (WoRMS AphiaID 517640) formally treats Pachyclavularia violacea as a superseded combination. The genus Briareum (family Briareidae, order Scleralcyonacea) includes both encrusting mat forms (B. violaceum — the classic Green Star Polyp) and branching/sea-stalk forms (B. asbestinum, common in the Caribbean). B. violaceum is the Indo-Pacific encrusting form most commonly sold in the hobby.

Growth form: B. violaceum forms low, flat, encrusting mats (stolons) studded with individual polyps that extend tentacles — typically 8 pinnate tentacles per polyp — giving a grass-like appearance when open. The stolon base tends to be purple-grey while polyp tips fluoresce green under actinic or blue LEDs. Polyps readily retract when disturbed but generally remain extended under stable conditions.

Spreading behavior: This coral tends to spread rapidly across any available hard substrate. In established reef tanks it has been observed to encrust rockwork, back walls, side panels, and overflow boxes. Physical barriers (e.g., placing frags on isolated rocks, or keeping a gap of bare sand between the colony and neighboring corals) are generally advised to prevent overgrowth. Despite the aggressive spreading, B. violaceum is considered peaceful in the sense that it lacks nematocysts capable of stinging adjacent corals; growth contact is the primary mechanism of aggression.

Lighting: Generally considered a moderate-light coral (roughly 75–150 PAR). Some sources indicate it tolerates low-light positions and will still grow, though color and polyp extension tend to be best under moderate intensity. Strong actinic/blue spectrum lighting tends to bring out fluorescent coloration. Notably, LiveAquaria lists B. asbestinum (branching sea-stalk form) as requiring 'high' intensity, which may reflect that form's different light adaptation.

Water parameters: B. violaceum is widely considered tolerant of imperfect water chemistry compared to SPS corals — a trait that contributes to its beginner reputation. However, stable NSW parameters (temperature 22–27°C, salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium ~400 ppm) are generally recommended for long-term health and polyp extension. Temperature source disagreement: LiveAquaria lists 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), WWC lists 72–76°F (22.2–24.4°C), and SaltwaterAquariumBlog lists 78–82°F (25.5–27.8°C). The averaged range across all three is approximately 22–27°C, adopted here; the spread does not meet the >2× factor requiring needs_review.

Fragging: Propagation is straightforward — place rubble or frag plugs in contact with the colony base and the mat encrusts the new substrate within days to weeks. Frags can then be separated by cutting the stolon with scissors or a blade. B. violaceum is one of the most frequently fragged and donated corals in the reef hobby.

Tank size: No minimum tank size is universally agreed upon; the coral itself is physically small enough for nano tanks but its spreading tendency means aquarists in small tanks may find management more demanding. A 20-gallon (76 L) minimum is commonly cited for keeping any reef system stable enough to host this coral without excessive maintenance burden.

Note: Some Briareum species (particularly B. asbestinum) have been reported to release toxic compounds when stressed or at death; while B. violaceum is not specifically implicated in published aquarium toxicity incidents, normal precautions (water change, carbon filtration if a large die-off occurs) are advisable.
breedingNotes: Fragging (cutting) via stolon separation is the standard captive propagation method. Place frag plugs or rubble in contact with an active colony edge; the mat encrusts the new substrate naturally. Cutting the stolon with clean scissors frees the new frag. Sexual reproduction occurs on wild reefs but is not practiced in captivity.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – Sea Stalk Briareum
primary.notes: LiveAquaria lists Briareum sp. (B. asbestinum sea-stalk form) with key care parameters. Used as primary source for salinity (1.023–1.025), pH (8.1–8.4), temperature (72–78°F / 22–26°C), and basic care level/feeding information.

additional.name: Saltwater Aquarium Blog – Green Star Polyps Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-check source for B. violaceum (listed as Pachyclavularia violacea). Confirmed care level Easy, alkalinity 8–12 dKH, calcium ~400 ppm, temperature 78–82°F (25.5–27.8°C), salinity 1.025, pH 8.2, moderate lighting and low-to-moderate flow. Spreading/overgrowth behavior documented. Temperature upper bound (27.8°C) is higher than LiveAquaria; averaged across sources to produce 22–27°C range adopted here.

additional.name: World Wide Corals – Green Star Polyps product
additional.notes: Confirmed care level Easy, lighting Medium, flow Low, temperature 72–76°F (22–24°C). Contributes to temperature range average.

additional.name: WoRMS – Briareum violaceum (AphiaID 517640)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy only: confirms Family Briareidae, Order Scleralcyonacea, and that Pachyclavularia violacea is a superseded combination of Briareum violaceum (Quoy & Gaimard, 1833). Native range records span Marshall Islands, Japanese, Chinese, and Taiwanese EEZs confirming Indo-Pacific distribution.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Briareum
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy cross-check only (Family Briareidae, Order Scleralcyonacea confirmed). Notes that aquarium specimens are frequently mislabeled as Pachyclavularia violacea. Not used for husbandry parameters.
